sentences.text: The Judenrat was, you see, like the-- like the government.
You know.
There was there like, everything-- everything-- the Germans' government-- the German-- the Nazis, the-- the Gestapo-- tell them to do, this they have to carry out.
Let's say they make a action, you know?
They say them-- they say to the Judenrat, you've got to bring 2,000 people on that place.
If not, you know-- you've got to do it.
This was a hard job for them to do.
Like, you've you got to [INAUDIBLE] your own brothers or your own sisters to kill.
That was the action.
If not, they will kill everybody.
And you've got to do it.
I remember the actions.
I remember the actions because when-- when there was action, I used to hide somewhere in the-- you know, on an attic somewhere because I know what could [? arrive ?].
If I go on there on that place, I know that I'll go and get killed.
Let's say they kill out-- they kill my father, my mother, my sisters, and my brothers.
They kill-- in the other country to kill them.
When I learned-- when my mother say, if you kill-- save yourself.
Hide somewhere.
We know-- we know where the killers are.
When I've been in the attic.
I've been in the attic when I my wife, too-- in the attic.
And after the action, we came down.
And we knew it.
We knew it-- they-- they killed in-- my mother, my father in Estonia.
And there they killed them.
What do you mean, what happens there in ghetto?
Right.
I don't remember the day.
But I-- I tell you, they took about 10,000.
And they know already the state of SS men.
The SS stay, and they-- and they select.
Right, left, right, left, right, left.
Who goes right is be alive.
Who go left, go-- get you killed.
And they select an amount from 10,000.
And they killed-- that's [INAUDIBLE].
They had the amount 10,000 to kill this day.
They get them to kill.
And the rest of them, they say, go back to the ghetto.
For the 10,000 people?
At that time, there was already graves for them.
And they put in one grave 10,000.
And this what we got every year a memorial for special-- for the 10,000 Jewish people.
And the monument is on the-- on the cemetery, for the 10,000, especially.
Yeah, yeah.
I was hiding.
Because when I-- how they got killed, I know how.
I've been-- I've been back three years ago.
I've been on a gathering from the survivors in Los Angeles.
They had the same thing, you know, what-- what you've got here-- what everybody told the stories to.
Some little, but not too much because it was a lot of people.
And when I meet people there-- what they came back alive in Estonia.
These people, they became alive.
Some of them ran away.
Some of them were hiding there.
And they say how they got killed.
They put them-- they put the stronger people-- they-- they kill them in the woods, you know.
And they got to take some, you know, wood.
To kill them, they put a line of wood on the [INAUDIBLE] but, let's say, about 50 people to lay down with their faces on the bottom.
And they shoot them.
And they put again wood.
And they put again 50 people.
And they shoot them.
And they put again till they make about-- they took with gasoline.
They shoot them, put gasoline, and they burned her up.
And this is the way they told me how my parents were between [? them ?] where they did it.
This is the reason I know.
In Estonia, no.
In Estonia, they killed all of them.
On the one thing-- my-- my youngest brother, he was a singer.
You know.
He was 13 years old.
He got to be bar mitzvah.
And that's when the Germans came with-- the same-- the same day that the bar mitzvah-- when the Germans come in to Lithuania.
He didn't have the bar mitzvah.
But he was a singer.
He used to sing solo by himself in-- in the biggest congregation.
Only he sang even in the ghetto.
Well, the Germans didn't kill him for nine months in Estonia.
They killed my parents.
Only he hid him-- the commandant from the ghetto-- he hid him for three months in-- in his inner room, like an animal, only to sing for him.
And later they found out-- the SS found out.
They killed him.
And the-- and-- and the-- the SS man, they arrested, you know, for loving the Jewish, for wanting to hide him.
But he got so emotional.
He sang for him, you know, ghetto songs and everything.
They say-- he sang for everybody.
In ghetto, I remember he sang something.
And everybody gave him a piece of bread for this.
Everybody kissed him.
Or gave him-- what can you give him?
A piece of bread only.
But there in Estonia, they kill my parents.
And he kept him for three months, the SS man.
He keeped him.
And the-- the-- later, the SS found out.
And they killed him on the-- on the-- I don't know what they did with him, the SS man.
You know.
I bet you they send him on the front or something like this.
Or that's-- or that's what they told me in Los Angeles and the gathering.
The last one, the last one-- brother, Berele.
You didn't have any-- in the ghetto, they was against-- you found-- you found a radio, you going to-- can get-- they can kill-- for one radio, they can kill a hundred-- a hundred people around.
There's-- there was a [INAUDIBLE].
If they found somewhere a radio or newspapers or something, this is killed.
That's dead.
I know somebody who escaped through the partisan.
You know.
You know what is the partisan?
A lot of them was underground, you know.
Partisan, where they go in the-- in the woods, you know, in the forests.
And they live and they-- and-- and-- and they fight the Nazis.
And this so they make bunkers, you know, in the woods.
And they live for years there.
I remember the names.
But-- but there's a lot of them, you know.
The main that I remember is Chaim Yelin.
Chaim Yelin was a writer, a Lithuanian writer.
He was my friend, [?
Clara's ?] friend.
And he begged me to go with him to the woods.
Before he went to the woods, he-- he led them-- he never shave.
He make him, like to old man, a beard with [INAUDIBLE], everything.
And he says to me, Sam, you got to do this, too, the same thing.
You've got to go in the-- in the underground.
If we don't go, we're going for the Axis.
You know, they're going to kill us.
But I couldn't leave my father with my mother with everything there.
I didn't go.
And he went.
And he went.
And later, he went on a mission and he got in a fight with the Germans.
When they ran after him, he killed some few Germans.
But he killed himself, too.
That's what-- the underground for the-- was with him together.
They told me this.
Oh, a second-- I got a book even from them.
They called the book, they call it
Before-- before the war?
No, the gas chambers, they make lately.
You see, they make-- they didn't have already so many to-- they want to make-- you know, like-- like, the world not to know about them.
They lately-- they make in 1944 or something like this.
They make the-- the-- they make the-- the gas chambers.
They make in Majdanek.
They make in Treblinka.
They make in Auschwitz.
They make in-- not in Dachau.
But they make a lot in Stutthof.
My wife was at Stutthof.
A gas chamber was there.
In the ghetto already, in the ghetto.
The last days in the ghetto.
We knew it now.
When they took-- they make an action on the cattles with the trains.
If you've been in Washington, on the-- in Washington museum, then you see the-- the trains were like carrying cattle.
There, when they were putting us-- I've been with my wife in those trains.
They carry me or my wife, hundreds of them, like-- like-- like cattle.
We were like cattle, without food, without water, without nothing in the world.
But I was stronger, like I am today.
While my wife was young and strong.
And they select who is-- who they like for them to work.
They select right now there.
They took the young ones-- to bring me in Dachau, my wife they carry to Stutthof.
And the rest of them, they put in Auschwitz and they burned him up.
I-- I don't remember the date.
And I don't--
The day-- but this-- I-- I-- one-- one year before the liberation, it was in 1943 or '44, is my guess.
I moved from-- they moved me from the ghetto in a-- in a-- in a hard labor camp.
You know.
How-- how I get work or something?
No, they told the-- they built a new camp for hard workers, you see.
And they took so many, or so many young people, young workers.
They took me.
They took my wife.
And they took somebody else, like, 200, 300, 400 people.
They say separate-- there's a separate camp for hard-- for hard labor, you know.
Schanz.
This was in Kovno.
Like-- like in Kovno, like Slobodka.
This is for-- like-- like-- like here is [INAUDIBLE], like here is-- Decatur, you know, this was Schanz.
I work in all kind of work.
Every day, some different work.
Some was plumbing, some was bricks, some was-- some was everything.
In Schanz we lived for something not quite a year, I think, about a year or something like this.
And later, the Russian-- the Russians started to come forward.
They occupied us.
They destroyed the-- the camp.
And they carried us about three days in Germany.
In Germany, there they separate me and they separate my wife.
Separate.
That's what I told you before.
They carry me in Dachau.
My wife they carry to Stutthof.
I didn't-- I-- I didn't marry, you know, legally because there was-- there was rumors in ghetto-- who were singles, the Germans are going to kill.
Who was-- who got married, who got a wife-- they want man and wife, you know, to be together that way.
We both-- we came in the Judenrat, like you say.
When I told them, I want to marry her, they gave me a piece of paper-- and married.
You see, only for the Germans.
They show us [?
I'm ?] married, but legally.
And before, I found my wife after the-- after the-- the war, after they liberate me, and after they liberate-- they also liberate my wife before me.
When I didn't know from her if she's alive.
And she didn't go from me if I'm alive.
But I found her.
They are-- the Russian army put-- make a list who they liberate.
And the American army make a list whom they liberate.
And they brought us together.
They found me on the list, and I found her.
And when we came in Germany, we got legally married.
About 500, or something like this.
The same-- what the Germans give to you, the piece of bread with-- with-- wiht a bowl of soup.
And--
Anything.
Some go into the woods to cut-- to cut-- to cut and to build something, and the bridge to make, everything-- to dig ditches, you know, everything.
My brother had the other camp like this.
They carry him, too, with his wife, you know, the same thing.
They make few different camps, you know.
Working, this was hard-working-- only young people they took.
The other one, they didn't.
The other one, they took the children, the young children, with the old people.
They burned them down and everything.
They burned them up in Auschwitz.
They killed them.
When?
In the-- after the war?
In camp.
That's where-- I didn't go nowhere.
I couldn't go nowhere.
They brought me-- they took me out from camp to work on the bombing camp.
I couldn't go out by myself.
Right.
That's what I told you before.
They took us in a train to carry everybody-- you know, who be-- whom they like, strong enough to work, like, to pick me up and send me in Dachau.
They pick up my wife.
And they send her to Stutthof.
That's a-- a camp.
And the rest of them, they carry them to Auschwitz.
When, when I've been in Dachau?
No, they carried our train to stop by Dachau.
There's these special trains, where they stop at Dachau, and special trains where they stop about the Stutthof.
Every-- every crematorium on every camp like Dachau, like Auschwitz, had the railroad, you know, right by them.
They came right with the trains to this-- to the ovens.
Yeah, sure.
A lot of friends, a lot of them, a lot of from ghetto, from our ghetto.
A lot of-- everybody was my friends.
When everybody go-- but a lot of-- till we came in Dachau.
Until we came in Stutthof, was already in train dead a lot.
Because without water, there was three days we-- they carry us in train.
Without food, without water, without nothing, and they didn't care.
It was a lot of people without toilets, without nothing.
Like-- like they came out like animals.
It was a lot of people-- what, they didn't move already.
You know, they was dead.
Yeah, yeah.
There was two years before liberation something like this.
In Dachau?
In Dachau, in the evening we came.
It was dark, in the evening.
We didn't know what [INAUDIBLE] in Dachau.
I don't-- everybody think they kill us right now in the gas chamber.
But-- but it is not-- was true.
We get out because everybody was young.
You know you get to work.
And the rest of them, they-- they went in Auschwitz.
They told us-- they gave you a place where to go in the barracks.
It was-- you are going, this is your place-- barracks number one, barracks number three, barracks number five.
So many, so many room barracks-- when I got to remember my barrack.
And they give you only a-- they give you a blanket.
And that's it.
And they give you-- later, they give you the-- they give you-- you got to-- the clothes.
You got take off all the clouds, naked.
And they give you the blue and white, you know, uniforms, the Dachau uniforms.
You know.
Everybody got to forget your name.
Nobody names were mentioned, only a number.
And they give you a number.
My number is there and there.
But-- but names, you forgot.
You don't have a name, you're told.
Nobody got a name here.
Here is a slave, slavery.
And-- and you've got a-- every slave got a number.
And that's it.
And they didn't care.
They give you a uniform.
They give you, let's say, a big one.
You know.
For a small man, they give you sometimes a big one.
For-- for a big man, they give you a small one.
They didn't care.
Only-- later, you got to change if you wanted with someone.
Who's big, you get the small.
You know.
The same way was like with shoes.
They give you a pair of shoes, now go.
You didn't-- you-- if you say you don't want it, you get shot.
You see, there is no such a thing you don't want it.
They give you this, and that's it.
Later, you got to find out somebody got a big foot or somebody got a small foot.
Oh, plenty were-- plenty were-- got shot.
Every-- there's nothing to-- every move, every move you're doing, you got shot.
There's no such a thing-- you're not-- they didn't have any punishment.
You shoot them for nothing sometimes.
When they come, the SS men-- when they come in ghetto, or maybe for-- for-- for a half a mile or something like this, you don't take and offer him the head.
You know, only [INAUDIBLE] take out and they're going to shoot you for this.
When a German sees you, you've got to take off your hat.
And when a German sees sometimes a-- a Jewish fellow-- a Jewish man don't have the right to go on the sidewalk.
You've got to going in the middle of the street.
And if you found you're going on the sidewalk, they shoot you.
There's no such a thing to-- to-- to do it.
What to see-- everybody see this.
And everybody knew it.
First of all, the second-- they post signs.
They put up signs.
If somebody don't follow the-- the regulations to go-- to go on the sidewalk or not to take off the hat or something like this, get shot, get killed.
No, this wasn't-- this was in the ghetto.
Because in Dachau, we didn't have any streets.
A lot of things, I told you, a lot of things.
And these are not special-- specially.
One day, we took-- they took us to the plumbing place.
One day, they took us to an airport, you know, to clean there everything.
One day, they took us to-- to-- in the factory sometimes to-- to care of heavy things, something like this.
Bunkers, you know, for the German airplanes, to build, or to carry cement.
You know.
Or like the took elderly people, they couldn't carry two bags at one time.
When they couldn't, they fall down.
And they shoot him.
About 4 o'clock in the morning, 4:00. 5 o'clock sometimes.
They make the appel.
You know.
You know appel?
This is everybody got to get up, or to stay without the hats.
It can be snow.
It can be rain, can be freezing.
You've got to stay and to wait.
They've got to count.
The Germans are coming, and they're counting.
How many people?
How many short?
And they take-- and they take us later to work.
And later, when you're coming back, counting again-- how many short?
On the-- on the when they carry us to the work, he got the report.
How many dead?
Back, you know.
How many alive are you bringing back?
And how many dead was there?
You've got to make, you know, a report, how-- to tell the SS how many-- on the places that we worked-- how many people died already, you know, were killed.
Like I told you, somebody-- an elderly man, he couldn't carry three bags cement.
He went down on the floor.
He shoot him.
And they laid him there like this.
And they got the report by the gate.
Again, the appel, you know, you're staying.
And you wait until you get the piece of bread, till you get the-- the-- bowl of soup or something that that's what they give them.
In the nighttime, when we come back from the work.
Only the morning, they give you a little piece of bread with something light.
They're calling this coffee, but it was [INAUDIBLE] coffee or something else.
I don't know what it is.
We didn't have [? all these things. ?]
Dark.
We didn't have-- we didn't have timing.
When you found-- you got [INAUDIBLE], he's dead.
We didn't have-- there's no such thing.
It was strict.
You know everything.
The guards?
The Germans?
Who-- who I can't know.
I remember only-- one thing that I remember.
When there was a-- it was about 30, 40 below zero.
We went to the work.
And a German-- the German soldiers scare us.
They scare us with the work.
And one man, he came back from the work, he found a potato on the street, a frozen potato, a little one.
And he hide this.
When he came through the gates, when the Germans see, he-- he hold something here.
He told him, what you got here?
He say, a potato, a frozen potato.
What-- he say, stay here with the potato until everybody had gone in.
And they took him by the-- by the-- by the wires, by the barbed wires by the wall.
And they told him to sit down like this, on the knees.
And they put-- and they put handcuffs on his hand.
And they took the frozen potato in his mouth.
And he stayed like this.
Overnight, in the frozen-- in the morning, he get up.
They found him dead with the potato in his mouth.
This is what I remember.
Yeah.
This was in my-- in my day.
No, I didn't get beat up because I've been lucky in them things.
You know.
I've been always with humor, you know, and everybody laugh whenever-- even-- even the Germans laugh sometimes what I did to them.
I used make sometimes-- one German, what he came into work every day.
It was Christmastime.
And he knew I do a lot of things.
He wanted to make him a doll from wood for his grandson.
And he wanted to give this for him a present.
And he come in the woods.
And I took a piece of wood.
And he gave me a piece of bread every day.
And he had sometimes a piece of meat or something like this from home, he give me-- you know, nobody to see.
Or sometimes he wrapped up in a piece of paper a-- a-- a-- a piece of chicken or something like this.
And he looked-- nobody, nobody Germans to see it what they're doing.
And he threw this is in the wood.
And he say, go, pick up.
Pick-- go, pick up.
You know.
And I said, come.
And in the wood, I open up, I see a piece of chicken.
And I sit down there.
And when I eat this-- what I did for him, a doll.
And I keep doing this for him for weeks, only to get time more.
You know, I didn't-- I didn't do the other work hard, only this what I did for him.
When I make a doll, I make up how much I can.
And-- and this, what he-- he give me sometimes a piece of bread.
Every day, he bring me something else.
But that's what I am lucky with.
But later on, I got-- have to-- near to the liberation, I got very sick.
And the Germans-- Germans took me away with all the sick, with all the dead people.
They think, you're-- I'm already dead.
And they put me in a big-- in a big train like this.
And then, later on, the-- the Americans-- they see already the Americans coming in, they throw out all the dead-- when you see on the picture there.
And they think I am dead, too, you know.
And they came to make movies, for the films, the dead-- thousands of thousands.
They found some of them moving, me and two more, me and two more.
Matter of fact, when I came here in Atlanta, I found them the third one, was-- was liberated the same like me.
I-- he was the third one.
We hugged each other.
We kissed each other here in Atlanta.
Anyway, but I didn't remember my liberation because I'd been in like, a coma, where the-- you know, if the American Army came with-- with a half hour later, I'd be already between the dead, you see.
And they tried to bring somebody-- somebody moving.
Maybe they tried to bring alive.
And they brought-- they took out me.
They took me like a piece of wood.
You know.
My weight was 39 pounds.
And they put me in the nearest hospital.
They call it Saint Ottilien, in a German hospital.
And-- and-- and they put me there.
And I-- I didn't remember this.
This was the-- the American Army.
They showed me where they liberate me.
They give me all the informations of how they found me.
When I opened up my eyes, I see around me soldiers and-- and nurses, white nurses.
And I've never see American uniforms-- you know, army.
I thought the Germans found out I'm alive.
And they came to-- they came to-- to finish me up.
When I start to holler, in German language, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Don't kill me, don't kill me, and I start-- and I said, don't kill me.
All the soldiers around me, I said, stay [INAUDIBLE].
And I holler, don't kill me!
And all of a sudden, came a-- a American-- a Jewish fellow, but older, like 50, 55, something like this, a major.
With tears in his eyes-- and he kissed me my head.
And he said, in Yiddish, in broken Yiddish, [SPEAKING YIDDISH], American [?
Yid. ?] My daughters are good [?
Yid. ?] My mama are good [?
Yid. ?]
